items.title: Exempt income expenses must be strictly exclude from book profits to compute MAT
items.summary: Eempt income, which were disallowed under Section 14A could not be automatically added back to compute the book profit for Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT) under Section 115JB without pinpointing real, actual expenditures recorded in the books of accounts by tax authorities that possess a direct nexus with the tax-free earnings. The post Exempt income expenses must be strictly exclude from book profits to compute MAT appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The concept of Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT) was introduced in the Income Tax Act, 1961, to ensure that companies pay a minimum amount of tax, irrespective of their taxable income. Section 115JB of the Act provides that where the income-tax payable on the total income of a company is less than 15% (plus surcharge and cess as applicable) of its book profit, the company shall be liable to pay tax at the rate of 15% (plus surcharge and cess as applicable) on such book profit. The book profit is calculated by making certain adjustments to the profit as per the profit and loss account, including disallowing expenses which are not deductible under the Act.

Section 14A of the Income Tax Act, 1961, deals with the disallowance of expenditure incurred in relation to income which is exempt from tax. The provision is aimed at preventing taxpayers from claiming a deduction for expenses incurred to earn exempt income, thereby reducing their taxable income. The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has also issued circulars and guidelines to clarify the scope and applicability of Section 14A.

The Supreme Court has also dealt with the issue of disallowance of expenses under Section 14A in various judgments, including the landmark case of Maxopp Investment Ltd. vs. Commissioner of Income Tax, where it was held that the disallowance under Section 14A is aimed at disallowing the expenditure which is incurred for earning the exempt income. The Court also emphasized the importance of maintaining proper records and accounts to substantiate the claim of expenses incurred for earning taxable income.

The Development in Detail

The recent development emphasizes the need to strictly exclude expenses related to exempt income from the book profits while computing MAT under Section 115JB. This means that expenses which are disallowed under Section 14A cannot be automatically added back to the book profit for MAT purposes without identifying the actual expenditures incurred in relation to the exempt income. The tax authorities must pinpoint the specific expenses which have a direct nexus with the tax-free earnings and disallow only those expenses.

This development is significant as it provides clarity on the computation of book profit for MAT purposes and ensures that companies are not unfairly taxed on account of expenses incurred for earning exempt income. It also underscores the importance of maintaining accurate and detailed records of expenses incurred in relation to both taxable and exempt income.

The development is also in line with the principles laid down by the Supreme Court in the Maxopp Investment Ltd. case, where it was held that the disallowance under Section 14A should be limited to the extent of expenses incurred for earning the exempt income. The Court's emphasis on the need for proper record-keeping and substantiation of expenses incurred for earning taxable income is also reinforced by this development.

Key Provisions & Impact

Section 115JB of the Income Tax Act, 1961, provides the framework for computing the book profit for MAT purposes. The section requires companies to make certain adjustments to their profit and loss account, including disallowing expenses which are not deductible under the Act. The recent development highlights the need to strictly exclude expenses related to exempt income from the book profits while computing MAT.

The impact of this development is significant, as it affects the computation of MAT liability for companies. Companies will need to ensure that they maintain accurate and detailed records of expenses incurred in relation to both taxable and exempt income. The development also underscores the importance of properly accounting for expenses incurred in relation to exempt income, to avoid disallowance of expenses under Section 14A.

The development is also expected to have a significant impact on the tax authorities' approach to assessing MAT liability. The tax authorities will need to carefully examine the expenses incurred by companies and identify the specific expenses which have a direct nexus with the tax-free earnings. This will require a more nuanced and detailed approach to assessing MAT liability, taking into account the specific facts and circumstances of each case.

Critical Analysis

The recent development highlights the complexities and challenges involved in computing MAT liability under Section 115JB. The need to strictly exclude expenses related to exempt income from the book profits while computing MAT underscores the importance of accurate and detailed record-keeping and accounting for expenses incurred in relation to both taxable and exempt income.

The development also raises important questions about the scope and applicability of Section 14A and its relationship with Section 115JB. The tax authorities will need to carefully consider the principles laid down by the Supreme Court in the Maxopp Investment Ltd. case and ensure that the disallowance of expenses under Section 14A is limited to the extent of expenses incurred for earning the exempt income.

The development is also significant from a policy perspective, as it highlights the need for a more nuanced and detailed approach to taxing companies. The government will need to consider the impact of the development on the overall tax regime and ensure that the tax laws are aligned with the principles of fairness, equity, and simplicity.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

Companies will need to ensure that they maintain accurate and detailed records of expenses incurred in relation to both taxable and exempt income. The following are some key action points for companies and tax practitioners:

1. Review and update accounting records to ensure that expenses incurred in relation to exempt income are properly accounted for and separated from expenses incurred for earning taxable income.

2. Ensure that the computation of book profit for MAT purposes is accurate and takes into account the strict exclusion of expenses related to exempt income.

3. Carefully examine the expenses incurred in relation to exempt income and identify the specific expenses which have a direct nexus with the tax-free earnings.

4. Consider seeking professional advice to ensure that the tax implications of the development are properly understood and addressed.

5. Review and update tax return filings to ensure that the computation of MAT liability is accurate and takes into account the recent development.

items.title: OSH Code 2020: India’s New Framework for Safer, Healthier & Dignified Workplaces
items.summary: Indias OSH Code 2020 introduces mandatory appointment letters and digital compliance systems to improve transparency and worker protection. The framework significantly increases employer accountability for workplace documentation and safety standards. The post OSH Code 2020: India’s New Framework for Safer, Healthier & Dignified Workplaces appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Occupational Safety, Health and Working Conditions Code, 2020 (OSH Code 2020) is a significant legislation aimed at regulating and ensuring the safety, health, and dignity of workers in India. The Code consolidates and amends various existing laws, including the Factories Act, 1948, and the Building and Other Construction Workers (Regulation of Employment and Conditions of Service) Act, 1996. The OSH Code 2020 is a crucial development in the realm of labour laws, and its provisions have far-reaching implications for employers, employees, and the overall work environment in India.

Section 2(z) of the OSH Code 2020 defines "worker" to include any person employed in an establishment, either directly or through a contractor, for the purpose of wages or other consideration. This broad definition encompasses a wide range of workers, including those in the formal and informal sectors. The Code also establishes the National Occupational Safety and Health Advisory Board, which is responsible for advising the Central Government on matters related to occupational safety and health.

The OSH Code 2020 is also significant in the context of the Constitution of India, particularly Article 21, which guarantees the right to life and personal liberty. The Supreme Court has consistently held that the right to a safe and healthy work environment is an integral part of the right to life (See: Consumer Education and Research Centre vs. Union of India, (1995) 3 SCC 42). The OSH Code 2020 is a legislative attempt to give effect to this constitutional mandate and ensure that workers are protected from hazardous working conditions.

The Development in Detail

The OSH Code 2020 introduces several key provisions aimed at improving workplace safety and health. One of the most significant developments is the mandatory appointment letter, which must be issued to all workers, including those employed through contractors. This provision is intended to provide workers with a clear understanding of their terms of employment, including their job description, wages, and benefits. The appointment letter must also include details of the worker's rights and obligations under the OSH Code 2020.

The OSH Code 2020 also establishes a digital compliance system, which requires employers to maintain electronic records of worker data, including details of their employment, wages, and working conditions. This system is designed to improve transparency and facilitate monitoring and enforcement of the Code's provisions. Employers are also required to display certain information, including the name and address of the establishment, the number of workers employed, and the contact details of the occupational safety and health officer.

The OSH Code 2020 also provides for the establishment of an internal complaint committee, which is responsible for addressing complaints related to occupational safety and health. The committee must include representatives of workers, employers, and government officials, and is required to maintain confidentiality and provide support to workers who report incidents or hazards.

Key Provisions & Impact

The OSH Code 2020 has significant implications for employers, who are now required to maintain detailed records of worker data and comply with stringent safety and health standards. The Code also increases employer accountability, as they are now liable for any accidents or injuries that occur in the workplace. Section 34 of the OSH Code 2020 provides that employers are responsible for ensuring the safety and health of workers, and may be penalized for non-compliance.

The OSH Code 2020 also has a positive impact on workers, who are now entitled to a safer and healthier work environment. The Code provides for regular health check-ups, training, and equipment to ensure that workers are protected from hazardous working conditions. Section 25 of the OSH Code 2020 requires employers to provide workers with personal protective equipment, and Section 30 requires them to maintain a safe and healthy work environment.

The OSH Code 2020 is also significant in the context of the gig economy and the increasing trend of contract work. The Code's provisions apply to all workers, including those employed through contractors, and provide them with similar rights and protections as regular employees. This development is likely to have far-reaching implications for the gig economy, as it increases the burden of compliance on employers and provides workers with greater protections and benefits.

Critical Analysis

The OSH Code 2020 is a significant development in the realm of labour laws, and its provisions have the potential to transform the work environment in India. However, the Code's effectiveness will depend on its implementation and enforcement. The government must ensure that employers comply with the Code's provisions, and that workers are aware of their rights and entitlements.

The OSH Code 2020 also raises several challenges, including the need for employers to maintain detailed records and comply with stringent safety and health standards. Small and medium-sized enterprises may struggle to comply with these requirements, and may require support and guidance to ensure that they meet the Code's provisions.

Despite these challenges, the OSH Code 2020 is a crucial step towards creating a safer and healthier work environment in India. The Code's provisions are based on international best practices, and are designed to promote a culture of safety and health in the workplace. The government must now ensure that the Code is implemented and enforced effectively, and that workers and employers are aware of their rights and obligations under the Code.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

Employers must take immediate action to comply with the OSH Code 2020's provisions, including issuing appointment letters to all workers and maintaining electronic records of worker data. They must also establish an internal complaint committee and ensure that workers are aware of their rights and entitlements under the Code.

The following are key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Ensure that all workers, including those employed through contractors, are issued appointment letters that include details of their terms of employment, wages, and benefits.

2. Maintain electronic records of worker data, including details of their employment, wages, and working conditions.

3. Establish an internal complaint committee to address complaints related to occupational safety and health, and ensure that workers are aware of their rights and entitlements under the Code.

4. Provide workers with regular health check-ups, training, and equipment to ensure that they are protected from hazardous working conditions.

5. Ensure that the workplace is safe and healthy, and that employers are aware of their obligations under the OSH Code 2020, including their liability for any accidents or injuries that occur in the workplace.

items.title: GSTN Introduces Major Changes in E-Way Bill System: Key Highlights
items.summary: GSTN has mandated the Ship To GSTIN field in Bill-To/Ship-To transactions to enhance traceability and reduce mismatches in goods movement. Businesses must now ensure accurate consignee details while generating e-Way Bills. The post GSTN Introduces Major Changes in E-Way Bill System: Key Highlights appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax Network (GSTN) has introduced significant changes to the e-Way Bill system, aimed at enhancing traceability and reducing mismatches in goods movement. This development is rooted in the provisions of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act), specifically Section 68, which empowers the Government to prescribe the manner of furnishing information relating to the movement of goods. Furthermore, Section 145A of the Income Tax Act, 1961, provides the framework for the electronic maintenance of records, which is crucial for the e-Way Bill system.

The e-Way Bill system, introduced under Rule 138 of the CGST Rules, 2017, requires the generation of an electronic waybill for the movement of goods valued above a certain threshold. The system is designed to track the movement of goods and prevent tax evasion. The GST Council, established under Article 279A of the Constitution, has been instrumental in shaping the GST framework, including the e-Way Bill system. The recent changes introduced by GSTN are a testament to the Council's efforts to refine the system and ensure seamless compliance.

The CGST Act, read with the IGST Act, provides the legislative framework for the e-Way Bill system. The provisions of Section 9(1) of the CGST Act, which deals with the levy of central tax, are also relevant in this context. The e-Way Bill system is an integral part of the GST framework, and its development is guided by the principles of simplicity, transparency, and accountability, as enshrined in the CGST Act.

The Development in Detail

The GSTN has mandated the Ship To GSTIN field in Bill-To/Ship-To transactions, effective immediately. This change is aimed at reducing mismatches in goods movement and enhancing traceability. The Ship To GSTIN field will now be a mandatory field, and businesses must ensure accurate consignee details while generating e-Way Bills. This development is a significant step towards streamlining the e-Way Bill system and preventing tax evasion.

The introduction of the mandatory Ship To GSTIN field is a logical extension of the e-Way Bill system. The system was designed to track the movement of goods and prevent tax evasion, and the mandatory field will help achieve these objectives. The GSTN has also provided guidance on the implementation of this change, including the necessary updates to the e-Way Bill portal. The development is a testament to the GSTN's commitment to refining the e-Way Bill system and ensuring seamless compliance.

The change is also expected to have a positive impact on the overall GST framework. By reducing mismatches in goods movement, the change will help prevent tax evasion and ensure that the correct amount of tax is paid. The development is also expected to simplify the compliance process for businesses, as they will no longer need to worry about mismatches in goods movement. The GSTN's efforts to refine the e-Way Bill system are a welcome step towards achieving the objectives of the GST framework.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

The GSTN has provided clarifications on the implementation of the mandatory Ship To GSTIN field. The field will be mandatory for all Bill-To/Ship-To transactions, and businesses must ensure accurate consignee details while generating e-Way Bills. The GSTN has also provided guidance on the necessary updates to the e-Way Bill portal, including the changes to the user interface and the backend systems.

The introduction of the mandatory Ship To GSTIN field is expected to have a significant impact on the e-Way Bill system. The change will help reduce mismatches in goods movement and enhance traceability, which will in turn help prevent tax evasion. The GSTN has also clarified that the change will not affect the existing e-Way Bills, and businesses can continue to generate e-Way Bills as usual. However, businesses must ensure that they update their systems to reflect the changes introduced by the GSTN.

The GSTN has also emphasized the importance of accurate consignee details in the e-Way Bill system. The Ship To GSTIN field is a critical component of the system, and businesses must ensure that they provide accurate details to avoid any discrepancies. The GSTN has also provided guidance on the consequences of non-compliance, including the penalties and fines that may be imposed for incorrect or incomplete information.

Critical Analysis

The introduction of the mandatory Ship To GSTIN field is a significant development in the e-Way Bill system. The change is expected to have a positive impact on the overall GST framework, as it will help reduce mismatches in goods movement and enhance traceability. The change will also simplify the compliance process for businesses, as they will no longer need to worry about mismatches in goods movement.

However, the change also raises some concerns. The mandatory Ship To GSTIN field may increase the compliance burden for small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs), which may not have the necessary resources or infrastructure to implement the change. The GSTN must provide adequate support and guidance to SMEs to ensure that they can comply with the change without any difficulties.

The introduction of the mandatory Ship To GSTIN field is also a testament to the GSTN's commitment to refining the e-Way Bill system. The GSTN has been working tirelessly to improve the system, and the change is a welcome step towards achieving the objectives of the GST framework. The GSTN must continue to monitor the system and make necessary changes to ensure that it remains effective and efficient.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The introduction of the mandatory Ship To GSTIN field has significant compliance implications for businesses. Businesses must ensure that they update their systems to reflect the changes introduced by the GSTN, including the mandatory Ship To GSTIN field. The following are some key action points for practitioners:

1. Update e-Way Bill systems to reflect the mandatory Ship To GSTIN field.

2. Ensure accurate consignee details while generating e-Way Bills to avoid any discrepancies.

3. Review existing e-Way Bill processes to ensure compliance with the new requirements.

4. Provide training to staff on the updated e-Way Bill system and the mandatory Ship To GSTIN field.

5. Monitor the e-Way Bill system regularly to ensure compliance and avoid any penalties or fines.

items.title: Assessment Void if Section 143(2) Notice Issued by Wrong Assessing Officer
items.summary: The article explains that a scrutiny assessment becomes invalid when the mandatory notice under Section 143(2) is issued by an officer lacking lawful pecuniary jurisdiction. It highlights that such jurisdictional defects cannot be cured through later transfer of the case. The post Assessment Void if Section 143(2) Notice Issued by Wrong Assessing Officer appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, provides a framework for the assessment of income tax in India. Section 143(2) of the Act is a crucial provision that empowers the Assessing Officer (AO) to issue a notice to the assessee, requiring them to attend the assessment proceedings and produce documents and information as may be required. This notice is a mandatory prerequisite for a scrutiny assessment, and its issuance is a critical step in the assessment process. The AO's jurisdiction to issue such a notice is determined by the provisions of Section 120 of the Act, which outlines the territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction of the AO.

The concept of jurisdiction is fundamental to the assessment process, and any defect in the AO's jurisdiction can render the entire assessment void. The Supreme Court has consistently held that the AO's jurisdiction is a condition precedent to the validity of the assessment proceedings. In the case of CIT v. Calcutta Discount Co. Ltd., the Court emphasized that the AO's jurisdiction is a threshold issue that must be satisfied before the assessment can proceed. Therefore, it is essential to examine the implications of a Section 143(2) notice issued by an AO lacking lawful pecuniary jurisdiction.

The CBDT has also issued circulars and guidelines to ensure that the AO's jurisdiction is properly exercised. For instance, Circular No. 14/2001, dated November 22, 2001, provides guidance on the transfer of cases between AOs and the implications on the assessment proceedings. These guidelines are essential in ensuring that the assessment process is fair, transparent, and in accordance with the provisions of the Act.

The Development in Detail

The recent development highlights the importance of ensuring that the Section 143(2) notice is issued by an AO with the requisite jurisdiction. If the notice is issued by an AO lacking lawful pecuniary jurisdiction, the assessment becomes invalid, and the entire process is vitiated. This is because the AO's jurisdiction is a fundamental aspect of the assessment process, and any defect in this regard cannot be cured through later transfer of the case. The tribunal has consistently held that the AO's jurisdiction is a condition precedent to the validity of the assessment proceedings, and any defect in this regard renders the assessment a nullity.

The implications of this development are far-reaching, and taxpayers must be aware of the risks associated with an invalid assessment. If the assessment is held to be invalid, the taxpayer may be entitled to seek a refund of the tax paid, along with interest and penalties. Furthermore, the taxpayer may also be entitled to claim a waiver of interest and penalties, as the assessment was invalid from the outset. However, the taxpayer must be vigilant and ensure that they challenge the assessment at the earliest opportunity, as delay can result in the loss of valuable rights.

The CBDT has also emphasized the importance of ensuring that the AO's jurisdiction is properly exercised. In the Instruction No. 5/2013, dated July 8, 2013, the CBDT instructed AOs to exercise caution while issuing notices and to ensure that the notice is issued within their jurisdiction. This instruction is a clear indication of the CBDT's commitment to ensuring that the assessment process is fair, transparent, and in accordance with the provisions of the Act.

Key Provisions & Impact

Section 143(2) of the Act is a critical provision that empowers the AO to issue a notice to the assessee, requiring them to attend the assessment proceedings and produce documents and information as may be required. The provision states that the AO shall serve a notice on the assessee, requiring them to attend the assessment proceedings, if the AO considers it necessary or expedient to do so. The notice must be issued within the time limit prescribed under Section 153 of the Act, and the AO must ensure that the notice is issued within their jurisdiction.

The impact of the development is significant, as it highlights the importance of ensuring that the AO's jurisdiction is properly exercised. If the notice is issued by an AO lacking lawful pecuniary jurisdiction, the assessment becomes invalid, and the entire process is vitiated. This can result in significant delays and costs for the taxpayer, as the assessment process must be restarted from the beginning. Furthermore, the taxpayer may also be entitled to claim a waiver of interest and penalties, as the assessment was invalid from the outset.

Section 120 of the Act outlines the territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction of the AO. The provision states that the AO shall have jurisdiction over the territory assigned to them by the CBDT, and they shall exercise their powers and functions in accordance with the provisions of the Act. The AO's jurisdiction is a critical aspect of the assessment process, and any defect in this regard can render the assessment a nullity. The CBDT has also issued guidelines to ensure that the AO's jurisdiction is properly exercised, and taxpayers must be aware of these guidelines to ensure that their rights are protected.

Critical Analysis

The development highlights the importance of ensuring that the AO's jurisdiction is properly exercised. The AO's jurisdiction is a fundamental aspect of the assessment process, and any defect in this regard can render the assessment a nullity. The tribunal has consistently held that the AO's jurisdiction is a condition precedent to the validity of the assessment proceedings, and any defect in this regard renders the assessment invalid. Therefore, it is essential to examine the implications of a Section 143(2) notice issued by an AO lacking lawful pecuniary jurisdiction.

The CBDT has also emphasized the importance of ensuring that the AO's jurisdiction is properly exercised. The CBDT has issued guidelines to ensure that the AO's jurisdiction is properly exercised, and taxpayers must be aware of these guidelines to ensure that their rights are protected. The Instruction No. 5/2013 is a clear indication of the CBDT's commitment to ensuring that the assessment process is fair, transparent, and in accordance with the provisions of the Act.

The development also highlights the importance of ensuring that the assessment process is fair, transparent, and in accordance with the provisions of the Act. The AO's jurisdiction is a critical aspect of the assessment process, and any defect in this regard can render the assessment a nullity. Therefore, it is essential to ensure that the AO's jurisdiction is properly exercised, and taxpayers must be aware of their rights and obligations under the Act. The CBDT must also ensure that the AO's jurisdiction is properly exercised, and the assessment process is fair, transparent, and in accordance with the provisions of the Act.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The development has significant compliance implications for taxpayers. Taxpayers must be aware of the risks associated with an invalid assessment and ensure that they challenge the assessment at the earliest opportunity. The taxpayer must also ensure that they maintain proper documentation and records to support their claim, as the onus of proof lies with the taxpayer. Furthermore, the taxpayer must also be aware of the guidelines issued by the CBDT and ensure that they comply with the provisions of the Act.

Here are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Ensure that the Section 143(2) notice is issued by an AO with the requisite jurisdiction, as any defect in this regard can render the assessment invalid.

2. Verify the AO's jurisdiction before responding to the notice, as any response to an invalid notice may be considered a waiver of the taxpayer's rights.

3. Maintain proper documentation and records to support the taxpayer's claim, as the onus of proof lies with the taxpayer.

4. Be aware of the guidelines issued by the CBDT and ensure that the taxpayer complies with the provisions of the Act.

5. Ensure that the taxpayer challenges the assessment at the earliest opportunity, as delay can result in the loss of valuable rights.

items.title: SC Upholds Bombay HC View That GST Cannot Be Levied on MIDC Plot Leasehold Assignment
items.summary: The Supreme Court refused to interfere with the Bombay High Court ruling that transfer of MIDC leasehold rights amounts to transfer of benefits arising from immovable property and is not liable to GST. The judgment also held that such assignment is not a supply of services under the CGST Act. The post SC Upholds Bombay HC View That GST Cannot Be Levied on MIDC Plot Leasehold Assignment appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime, introduced by the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act), and the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (IGST Act), has brought about a paradigm shift in the indirect taxation landscape of India. The CGST Act defines "supply" as the supply of goods or services or both, made or agreed to be made for a consideration by a person in the course or furtherance of business. Section 9(1) of the CGST Act provides that the Central Tax shall be levied on all intra-state supplies of goods or services or both at the rates specified in the Schedules appended to the Act. However, the levy of GST on certain transactions, such as the transfer of leasehold rights, has been a subject of controversy and litigation.

The Maharashtra Industrial Development Corporation (MIDC) allots plots of land to industrial units on a leasehold basis, and the transfer of such leasehold rights is a common practice. The question that has arisen is whether such transfer of leasehold rights is liable to GST. The CGST Act provides that the transfer of goods or services or both shall be considered a supply, but it does not specifically deal with the transfer of leasehold rights. The issue has been addressed by the courts, and the recent judgment of the Supreme Court upholding the Bombay High Court's view that GST cannot be levied on MIDC plot leasehold assignment is a significant development in this regard.

Section 2(17) of the CGST Act defines "business" to include any trade, commerce, manufacture, profession, vocation, adventure, wager, or any other similar activity, whether or not it is for a pecuniary benefit. The term "supply" is defined under Section 2(108) of the CGST Act to include all forms of supply of goods or services or both, such as sale, transfer, barter, exchange, license, rental, lease, or disposal made or agreed to be made for a consideration by a person in the course or furtherance of business. However, the Act does not specifically provide for the treatment of transfer of leasehold rights, and the courts have had to interpret the provisions to determine the liability of GST on such transactions.

The Development in Detail

The Supreme Court's judgment upholding the Bombay High Court's view that GST cannot be levied on MIDC plot leasehold assignment is a significant development in the context of GST litigation. The Bombay High Court had held that the transfer of MIDC leasehold rights amounts to the transfer of benefits arising from immovable property and is not liable to GST. The court observed that the transfer of leasehold rights is not a supply of services under the CGST Act, as it does not involve the provision of any services. The Supreme Court has now upheld this view, refusing to interfere with the High Court's ruling.

The judgment is based on the interpretation of the CGST Act and the definition of "supply" under Section 2(108) of the Act. The court has held that the transfer of leasehold rights does not constitute a supply of goods or services or both, as it does not involve the provision of any goods or services. The court has also relied on the definition of "business" under Section 2(17) of the CGST Act, which includes any trade, commerce, manufacture, profession, vocation, adventure, wager, or any other similar activity, whether or not it is for a pecuniary benefit. The court has observed that the transfer of leasehold rights is not an activity that is carried out in the course or furtherance of business, and therefore, it is not liable to GST.

The judgment has significant implications for the industry, as it provides clarity on the treatment of transfer of leasehold rights under the GST regime. The judgment is also a testament to the judiciary's role in interpreting the provisions of the GST Act and providing guidance to the taxpayers and the tax authorities. The Supreme Court's judgment is expected to have a far-reaching impact on the GST litigation landscape, and it is likely to influence the outcome of similar cases that are pending before the courts.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

Section 2(17) of the CGST Act defines "business" to include any trade, commerce, manufacture, profession, vocation, adventure, wager, or any other similar activity, whether or not it is for a pecuniary benefit. The term "supply" is defined under Section 2(108) of the CGST Act to include all forms of supply of goods or services or both, such as sale, transfer, barter, exchange, license, rental, lease, or disposal made or agreed to be made for a consideration by a person in the course or furtherance of business. However, the Act does not specifically provide for the treatment of transfer of leasehold rights, and the courts have had to interpret the provisions to determine the liability of GST on such transactions.

The Supreme Court's judgment upholding the Bombay High Court's view that GST cannot be levied on MIDC plot leasehold assignment provides clarity on the treatment of transfer of leasehold rights under the GST regime. The judgment is based on the interpretation of the CGST Act and the definition of "supply" under Section 2(108) of the Act. The court has held that the transfer of leasehold rights does not constitute a supply of goods or services or both, as it does not involve the provision of any goods or services. The court has also relied on the definition of "business" under Section 2(17) of the CGST Act, which includes any trade, commerce, manufacture, profession, vocation, adventure, wager, or any other similar activity, whether or not it is for a pecuniary benefit.

The judgment is consistent with the principles of GST, which aims to tax the supply of goods and services or both. The transfer of leasehold rights is not a supply of goods or services or both, as it does not involve the provision of any goods or services. The judgment is also consistent with the definition of "business" under the CGST Act, which includes any trade, commerce, manufacture, profession, vocation, adventure, wager, or any other similar activity, whether or not it is for a pecuniary benefit. The transfer of leasehold rights is not an activity that is carried out in the course or furtherance of business, and therefore, it is not liable to GST.

Critical Analysis

The Supreme Court's judgment upholding the Bombay High Court's view that GST cannot be levied on MIDC plot leasehold assignment is a significant development in the context of GST litigation. The judgment provides clarity on the treatment of transfer of leasehold rights under the GST regime and is consistent with the principles of GST, which aims to tax the supply of goods and services or both. The judgment is also consistent with the definition of "business" under the CGST Act, which includes any trade, commerce, manufacture, profession, vocation, adventure, wager, or any other similar activity, whether or not it is for a pecuniary benefit.

However, the judgment may have implications for the revenue collection of the government, as it may result in a loss of revenue for the exchequer. The government may need to revisit the provisions of the CGST Act and consider amendments to clarify the treatment of transfer of leasehold rights under the GST regime. The judgment may also have implications for the industry, as it may result in a reduction in the cost of transfer of leasehold rights, which may lead to an increase in the demand for such transactions.

The judgment is a testament to the judiciary's role in interpreting the provisions of the GST Act and providing guidance to the taxpayers and the tax authorities. The Supreme Court's judgment is expected to have a far-reaching impact on the GST litigation landscape, and it is likely to influence the outcome of similar cases that are pending before the courts. The judgment is also a reminder of the importance of clarity and certainty in tax laws, which is essential for the smooth functioning of the tax system and for promoting economic growth and development.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The Supreme Court's judgment upholding the Bombay High Court's view that GST cannot be levied on MIDC plot leasehold assignment has significant implications for compliance and action points for taxpayers and tax practitioners. The following are some of the key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Taxpayers who have transferred leasehold rights may not be required to pay GST on such transactions, and they may be eligible for a refund of any GST paid on such transactions.

2. Taxpayers who are planning to transfer leasehold rights should ensure that they comply with the provisions of the CGST Act and the definition of "supply" under Section 2(108) of the Act.

3. Tax practitioners should review the provisions of the CGST Act and the definition of "business" under Section 2(17) of the Act to ensure that they are advising their clients correctly on the treatment of transfer of leasehold rights under the GST regime.

items.title: Delhi HC Refuses GST Refund Writ as Effective Statutory Appeal Before GSTAT Was Available
items.summary: Delhi High Court held that disputes relating to GST refund rejection and appreciation of evidence should be pursued through the statutory appellate remedy under Section 112 of the CGST Act. The Court declined writ interference as GSTAT was functional and operational. The post Delhi HC Refuses GST Refund Writ as Effective Statutory Appeal Before GSTAT Was Available appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act) provides a statutory framework for the administration of goods and services tax (GST) in India. Section 54 of the CGST Act deals with the refund of tax, interest, penalty, fees, or any other amount paid by an applicant. The refund claim can be made under various provisions, including excess payment of tax, export of goods or services, or on account of issuance of refund vouchers. However, the refund claim is subject to certain conditions and limitations, and the proper officer may reject the refund claim after following the due process of law. In such cases, the applicant has the right to appeal against the rejection order before the Appellate Authority, and further before the Appellate Tribunal, as per Section 107 and Section 112 of the CGST Act, respectively.

The GST Appellate Tribunal (GSTAT) has been established under Section 109 of the CGST Act to hear appeals against the orders passed by the Appellate Authority. The GSTAT is a quasi-judicial body that provides an effective and efficient mechanism for resolving disputes related to GST. The establishment of GSTAT is a significant step towards providing a statutory appellate remedy to taxpayers, ensuring that their grievances are redressed in a fair and timely manner. The CGST Act also provides for the National Appellate Authority for Advance Ruling (NAAR) under Section 101A, which hears appeals against the advance rulings pronounced by the Authority for Advance Ruling (AAR). The NAAR and GSTAT are crucial institutions in the GST appellate hierarchy, enabling taxpayers to seek redressal of their grievances through a structured and time-bound process.

In the context of GST refund claims, the statutory appellate remedy plays a vital role in ensuring that taxpayers' rights are protected. The appellate mechanism provides an opportunity for taxpayers to challenge the rejection of refund claims and seek relief against any adverse orders passed by the proper officer. The appellate authorities, including the Appellate Authority and the GSTAT, are empowered to examine the evidence, appreciate the facts, and apply the law to arrive at a just and fair decision. This process helps to ensure that the rights of taxpayers are safeguarded, and any errors or injustices are corrected through a fair and transparent process.

The Development in Detail

Recently, the Delhi High Court had the opportunity to examine the issue of GST refund claims and the availability of statutory appellate remedy in a writ petition filed by a taxpayer. The taxpayer had claimed a refund of GST paid on certain supplies, but the proper officer rejected the refund claim. The taxpayer then approached the Delhi High Court, seeking a writ of mandamus to direct the proper officer to grant the refund. However, the High Court declined to entertain the writ petition, holding that the taxpayer had an effective statutory appellate remedy available before the GSTAT.

The Court observed that the GSTAT was functional and operational, providing an effective mechanism for resolving disputes related to GST refund claims. The Court noted that the taxpayer had not availed of the statutory appellate remedy, despite having the opportunity to do so. The Court held that the writ jurisdiction should not be exercised when an effective statutory appellate remedy is available, as it would undermine the statutory framework and the institutional mechanism established for resolving disputes. The Court's decision emphasizes the importance of exhausting the statutory appellate remedy before seeking writ relief, ensuring that the taxpayer has availed of all available avenues of redressal before approaching the High Court.

The Delhi High Court's decision is significant, as it highlights the importance of the statutory appellate mechanism in resolving GST disputes. The Court's emphasis on the availability of an effective statutory appellate remedy before the GSTAT underscores the need for taxpayers to exhaust all available avenues of redressal before seeking writ relief. This approach ensures that the statutory framework is respected, and the institutional mechanism established for resolving disputes is allowed to function effectively. By declining to entertain the writ petition, the Court has reinforced the importance of the appellate process in resolving GST disputes, providing a clear signal to taxpayers and tax authorities alike.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

Section 112 of the CGST Act provides for the establishment of the GSTAT, which has the power to hear appeals against the orders passed by the Appellate Authority. The GSTAT is a quasi-judicial body that provides an effective and efficient mechanism for resolving disputes related to GST. The CGST Act also provides for the National Appellate Authority for Advance Ruling (NAAR) under Section 101A, which hears appeals against the advance rulings pronounced by the Authority for Advance Ruling (AAR). The NAAR and GSTAT are crucial institutions in the GST appellate hierarchy, enabling taxpayers to seek redressal of their grievances through a structured and time-bound process.

Section 107 of the CGST Act provides for the appeal to the Appellate Authority, which is the first level of appeal against the orders passed by the proper officer. The Appellate Authority has the power to hear appeals against the orders passed by the proper officer, and its decisions can be further appealed before the GSTAT. The CGST Act also provides for the revisional powers of the Commissioner under Section 120, which enables the Commissioner to revise the orders passed by the proper officer. However, the revisional powers are limited, and the Commissioner can only revise the orders in certain circumstances, such as when the order is erroneous or prejudicial to the interests of revenue.

The CGST Act provides a structured appellate mechanism for resolving GST disputes, ensuring that taxpayers have access to an effective and efficient process for redressal of their grievances. The statutory appellate remedy provides an opportunity for taxpayers to challenge the orders passed by the proper officer, and the appellate authorities have the power to examine the evidence, appreciate the facts, and apply the law to arrive at a just and fair decision. The appellate mechanism is designed to ensure that the rights of taxpayers are protected, and any errors or injustices are corrected through a fair and transparent process.

Critical Analysis

The Delhi High Court's decision to refuse the writ petition and direct the taxpayer to avail of the statutory appellate remedy before the GSTAT is a significant development in the realm of GST dispute resolution. The Court's emphasis on the availability of an effective statutory appellate remedy underscores the importance of exhausting all available avenues of redressal before seeking writ relief. This approach ensures that the statutory framework is respected, and the institutional mechanism established for resolving disputes is allowed to function effectively.

The decision highlights the importance of the appellate process in resolving GST disputes, providing a clear signal to taxpayers and tax authorities alike. The GSTAT, as a quasi-judicial body, has the power to hear appeals against the orders passed by the Appellate Authority, providing an effective and efficient mechanism for resolving disputes related to GST. The NAAR and GSTAT are crucial institutions in the GST appellate hierarchy, enabling taxpayers to seek redressal of their grievances through a structured and time-bound process.

The decision also emphasizes the need for taxpayers to be aware of the statutory appellate mechanism and to avail of the available remedies before seeking writ relief. Taxpayers should be cautious in approaching the High Court, as the Court is likely to exercise its writ jurisdiction only in exceptional circumstances, such as when there is a clear violation of fundamental rights or a patent error of law. The decision reinforces the importance of the appellate process in resolving GST disputes, ensuring that taxpayers have access to an effective and efficient mechanism for redressal of their grievances.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The Delhi High Court's decision has significant compliance implications for taxpayers, who should be aware of the statutory appellate mechanism and avail of the available remedies before seeking writ relief. Taxpayers should ensure that they have exhausted all available avenues of redressal before approaching the High Court, as the Court is likely to exercise its writ jurisdiction only in exceptional circumstances.

Here are some key takeaways for practitioners: 1. Taxpayers should ensure that they have availed of the statutory appellate remedy before the Appellate Authority and the GSTAT, as the case may be, before seeking writ relief. 2. Taxpayers should be aware of the time limits for filing appeals before the Appellate Authority and the GSTAT, and ensure that they file their appeals within the prescribed time limits. 3. Taxpayers should ensure that they have provided all relevant documentation and evidence to support their appeal, as the appellate authorities will examine the evidence and apply the law to arrive at a just and fair decision. 4. Taxpayers should be cautious in approaching the High Court, as the Court is likely to exercise its writ jurisdiction only in exceptional circumstances, such as when there is a clear violation of fundamental rights or a patent error of law. 5. Taxpayers should seek the advice of a tax professional or a lawyer to ensure that they have availed of the available remedies and have complied with the statutory requirements, as the appellate process can be complex and time-consuming.

By following these action points, taxpayers can ensure that they have availed of the available remedies and have complied with the statutory requirements, minimizing the risk of adverse orders and ensuring that their rights are protected. The decision of the Delhi High Court emphasizes the importance of the appellate process in resolving GST disputes, and taxpayers should be aware of the compliance implications and take necessary steps to ensure that they are in compliance with the statutory requirements.

items.title: P&H HC Quashes Income Tax Demand Not Included in Approved IBC Resolution Plan

items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (IBC) has been a significant development in the Indian legal landscape, aiming to consolidate and amend the laws relating to reorganization and insolvency resolution of corporate persons. Section 31 of the IBC provides that once a resolution plan is approved by the Adjudicating Authority, it shall be binding on all stakeholders, including the Central Government, any State Government, or any local authority. This provision has far-reaching implications, particularly in the context of tax demands. The Income Tax Act, 1961, also provides for the recovery of tax demands, but the interplay between the two statutes has been a subject of debate.

Section 238 of the IBC provides that the provisions of the IBC shall have effect notwithstanding anything inconsistent therewith contained in any other law for the time being in force. This provision suggests that the IBC shall override other laws, including the Income Tax Act, in cases of inconsistency. However, the extent of this override has been a subject of interpretation. The CBDT has issued circulars and notifications to clarify the position, but the issue remains contentious.

The Supreme Court, in the case of Innoventive Industries Ltd. v. ICICI Bank, has held that the IBC is a complete code in itself, providing for the insolvency resolution process. The Court also emphasized the importance of maximizing the value of assets and balancing the interests of all stakeholders. This judgment has been significant in shaping the jurisprudence on the IBC and its interaction with other laws, including the Income Tax Act.

The Development in Detail

The recent judgment of the Punjab and Haryana High Court has added a new dimension to the debate. The Court held that once a resolution plan is approved under Section 31 of the IBC, tax demands not included in the plan stand extinguished. The Court set aside the assessment order, demand notice, and penalty proceedings, providing relief to the taxpayer. This judgment is significant, as it clarifies the position on the treatment of tax demands in the context of the IBC.

The Court's reasoning was based on the provisions of Section 31 of the IBC, which provides that the approved resolution plan shall be binding on all stakeholders. The Court held that this provision implies that all debts, including tax demands, shall be extinguished, unless specifically provided for in the resolution plan. The Court also relied on the principle of pari passu, which requires that all creditors be treated equally and without preference.

The judgment has significant implications for taxpayers and revenue authorities alike. It provides clarity on the treatment of tax demands in the context of the IBC and underscores the importance of ensuring that tax demands are properly provided for in the resolution plan. The judgment also highlights the need for coordination between the revenue authorities and the stakeholders in the insolvency resolution process.

Key Provisions & Impact

The provision states that the resolution plan shall be binding on all stakeholders, including the Central Government, any State Government, or any local authority. Section 238 of the IBC provides that the provisions of the IBC shall have effect notwithstanding anything inconsistent therewith contained in any other law for the time being in force. This provision suggests that the IBC shall override other laws, including the Income Tax Act, in cases of inconsistency. The CBDT has issued circulars and notifications to clarify the position, but the issue remains contentious.

The judgment of the Punjab and Haryana High Court has significant implications for the revenue authorities. It underscores the importance of ensuring that tax demands are properly provided for in the resolution plan. The judgment also highlights the need for coordination between the revenue authorities and the stakeholders in the insolvency resolution process. The revenue authorities must ensure that they are properly represented in the committee of creditors and that their interests are protected.

Critical Analysis

The judgment of the Punjab and Haryana High Court is a significant development in the context of the IBC and its interaction with the Income Tax Act. The judgment provides clarity on the treatment of tax demands in the context of the IBC and underscores the importance of ensuring that tax demands are properly provided for in the resolution plan. However, the judgment also raises several questions, including the scope of Section 31 of the IBC and the extent of the override provided by Section 238.

The judgment highlights the need for coordination between the revenue authorities and the stakeholders in the insolvency resolution process. The revenue authorities must ensure that they are properly represented in the committee of creditors and that their interests are protected. The judgment also underscores the importance of ensuring that tax demands are properly provided for in the resolution plan, to avoid disputes and litigation.

The judgment is also significant from a policy perspective. It highlights the need for a clear and consistent policy on the treatment of tax demands in the context of the IBC. The government must ensure that the revenue authorities are properly equipped to deal with the challenges posed by the IBC and that the interests of the revenue are protected. The judgment is a significant step in the right direction, but more needs to be done to ensure that the IBC is implemented effectively and that the interests of all stakeholders are protected.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The judgment of the Punjab and Haryana High Court has significant implications for taxpayers and revenue authorities alike. Taxpayers must ensure that they are properly represented in the committee of creditors and that their interests are protected. The revenue authorities must ensure that they are properly represented in the committee of creditors and that their interests are protected. The following are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Ensure that tax demands are properly provided for in the resolution plan, to avoid disputes and litigation.

2. Ensure that the revenue authorities are properly represented in the committee of creditors, to protect their interests.

3. Ensure that the resolution plan is properly approved by the Adjudicating Authority, to avoid challenges and disputes.

items.title: Bombay HC Orders Rehearing in GST Demand Raised against Son After Taxpayer’s Death
items.summary: The Bombay High Court granted a fresh hearing where GST recovery proceedings were initiated against the son for alleged tax dues of his deceased father. The Court held that all contentions, including challenge to statutory provisions, would remain open. The post Bombay HC Orders Rehearing in GST Demand Raised against Son After Taxpayer’s Death appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The GST legislation, specifically the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act), and the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (IGST Act), outline the framework for the recovery of tax dues. Section 79 of the CGST Act deals with the recovery of tax, interest, fine, or penalty. This section empowers the proper officer to recover the amount due from a taxable person as if it were an arrear of land revenue. Furthermore, Section 122 of the CGST Act provides for the punishment for certain offences, including the evasion of tax, which can lead to the initiation of recovery proceedings.

In the context of the deceased taxpayer, the relevant provision is Section 149 of the CGST Act, which deals with the recovery of tax from the estate of the deceased. This section states that where a person dies, the tax payable by such person under the Act shall be a first charge on the property of the deceased. Moreover, Section 160 of the CGST Act provides for the liability of the legal representatives of the deceased person to pay any sum which is a charge on the estate of the deceased person.

It is also relevant to consider the principles of natural justice and the right to a fair hearing, as enshrined in the Constitution of India, particularly Article 14 and Article 21. These principles are fundamental to the administration of justice and have been upheld in numerous Supreme Court and High Court judgments. For instance, the Supreme Court in the case of Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978) 1 SCC 248, emphasized the importance of a fair hearing in the context of administrative decisions.

The Development in Detail

The recent order of the Bombay High Court, directing a rehearing in a GST demand raised against the son of a deceased taxpayer, is significant. The Court's decision to allow a fresh hearing, where all contentions, including the challenge to statutory provisions, would remain open, underscores the importance of ensuring that the principles of natural justice are upheld. This approach is consistent with the established jurisprudence that a person against whom a recovery proceeding is initiated must be given an opportunity to contest the claim.

The facts of the case, as reported, indicate that the GST recovery proceedings were initiated against the son for alleged tax dues of his deceased father. The son, being the legal representative of the deceased taxpayer, is potentially liable for the tax dues under Section 160 of the CGST Act. However, the Court's order suggests that the son was not afforded an adequate opportunity to respond to the allegations and contest the demand.

The Court's decision to grant a rehearing in this matter highlights the need for the tax authorities to follow the principles of natural justice and ensure that all parties are given a fair opportunity to present their case. This approach is essential to maintaining the integrity of the tax administration system and upholding the rule of law. As observed by the Supreme Court in Smt. Ujjam Bai v. State of Uttar Pradesh (1962) 1 SCR 294, the principles of natural justice are an essential part of the concept of justice and must be followed in all administrative decisions.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

Section 160 of the CGST Act is a crucial provision in this context, as it deals with the liability of the legal representatives of the deceased person to pay any sum which is a charge on the estate of the deceased person. This section must be read in conjunction with Section 149 of the CGST Act, which provides that the tax payable by a person under the Act shall be a first charge on the property of the deceased.

The CGST Act also provides for the issuance of a show-cause notice under Section 73 or Section 74, as the case may be, before initiating recovery proceedings. This notice is a critical component of the recovery process, as it provides the taxpayer with an opportunity to respond to the allegations and contest the demand. The tax authorities must ensure that the notice is properly served and that the taxpayer is given adequate time to respond.

Furthermore, the tax authorities must also consider the provisions of the Limitation Act, 1963, which govern the time limits for initiating recovery proceedings. Section 48 of the CGST Act provides that the provisions of the Limitation Act shall apply to the proceedings under the Act, subject to the provisions of the Act. The tax authorities must ensure that they are aware of the applicable time limits and initiate recovery proceedings within the prescribed period.

Critical Analysis

The Bombay High Court's order directing a rehearing in the GST demand raised against the son of a deceased taxpayer is a significant development. The Court's approach underscores the importance of upholding the principles of natural justice and ensuring that all parties are given a fair opportunity to present their case. This decision is consistent with the established jurisprudence that a person against whom a recovery proceeding is initiated must be given an opportunity to contest the claim.

The tax authorities must take note of this decision and ensure that they follow the principles of natural justice in all recovery proceedings. This includes providing the taxpayer with adequate notice and an opportunity to respond to the allegations, as well as ensuring that the taxpayer is given a fair hearing. The tax authorities must also be aware of the applicable time limits and initiate recovery proceedings within the prescribed period.

The decision also highlights the need for the tax authorities to be mindful of the provisions of the CGST Act, particularly Section 160, which deals with the liability of the legal representatives of the deceased person. The tax authorities must ensure that they are aware of the applicable provisions and follow the correct procedure when initiating recovery proceedings against the legal representatives of a deceased taxpayer.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The Bombay High Court's order has significant compliance implications for taxpayers and tax practitioners. The following are some key takeaways:

1. Ensure adequate notice and opportunity to respond: Taxpayers must ensure that they receive adequate notice and an opportunity to respond to any allegations or demands made by the tax authorities.

2. Be aware of the applicable time limits: Taxpayers and tax practitioners must be aware of the applicable time limits for initiating recovery proceedings and ensure that they are aware of the prescribed period for responding to any notices or demands.

3. Understand the provisions of Section 160 of the CGST Act: Taxpayers and tax practitioners must understand the provisions of Section 160 of the CGST Act, which deals with the liability of the legal representatives of the deceased person.

4. Ensure proper documentation and record-keeping: Taxpayers and tax practitioners must ensure that they maintain proper documentation and records, including records of all notices, demands, and responses.

5. Seek legal advice and representation: Taxpayers and tax practitioners must seek legal advice and representation in all recovery proceedings to ensure that their rights are protected and that they receive a fair hearing.
